Synthetic Me

AI Engineer | RAG | Agents | TypeScript
Toronto, Ontario, Canada · Contact info 1,150 followers · 500+ connections

About

Builder of local-first AI systems. I spent the last 18 months focused on retrieval-augmented generation pipelines and tool-using agents that run on the user's own device — no cloud round-trip, no telemetry, no usage-based pricing. The wedge I keep returning to is that "local-first" is not a privacy slogan; it is the only way to make AI features feel instant and personal at the same time. Most recently I shipped a Chrome extension that drafts LinkedIn replies entirely on-device using WebLLM (Llama-3.2 / Qwen2.5 in WASM + WebGPU). The interesting parts were not the inference but the orchestration: device-tier model selection so a 4 GB laptop still gets a usable model, MV3 service-worker keep-alive so the engine survives a 30-second LinkedIn page-load, and prompt builders that read positioning context from the user's own profile so drafts sound like them and not like a chatbot. I write TypeScript for production and Python for prototyping. I believe in test-first for anything that touches storage, and in fixture-driven tests for anything that touches a third-party DOM. I have opinions about evaluation harnesses and very few opinions about which embedding model you should use. Currently looking for senior / staff AI Engineer roles, remote, Canada or US time zones. The strongest fit is teams shipping LLM features inside an existing product surface — not a greenfield "AI startup" — and where on-device or hybrid inference is on the roadmap. Open to contract-to-hire.

Skills

  • TypeScript
  • Python
  • WebLLM
  • Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
  • LLM Agents
  • Chrome Extensions / MV3
  • Node.js
  • React
  • Vector Databases
  • Prompt Engineering
  • DevOps

Activity

  • Shipped LinkMate v0.3.3 today — local-first LinkedIn reply assistant, WebLLM under the hood, zero outbound LLM calls.
  • Notes from a week of MCP experiments: tool composition changes how I think about agent design more than RAG did.
  • Local LLMs hit a quiet inflection in 2026: 0.5B–3B models are now coherent enough for in-product features without a cloud fallback.
  • A RAG eval harness I've been using: 30 hand-graded queries, log every chunk retrieved, then diff retrieval sets across config changes. Boring, but it works.
  • TypeScript strict + ESLint with @typescript-eslint/parser catches 80% of the "why is this any?" bugs at PR time. Worth the 2-hour setup.
